message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="steve-schoger" color="pink" summary="Usability review of component design decisions">
Hey Steve, Steve Schoger here -- the designer. I want to run my component design thinking past you for a usability gut-check before we start building features.

Key design decisions I'd like your take on:

1. **Auth forms**: Centered, narrow, single-column. Email, password, submit. Toggle between "Sign in" and "Create account" via a text link below the form. Inline error messages directly below the relevant field. Does this pass the "Don't Make Me Think" test?

2. **Empty state**: When a user registers and sees their todo list for the first time, it's empty. I'm planning a centered muted message like "No todos yet" with the add-todo input prominently at the top. Is that enough to guide a first-time user, or do we need something more?

3. **Delete action**: I'm making the delete button only visible on hover/focus-within on each todo item. This keeps the list clean but the action is discoverable. On mobile, it would need to be always visible since there's no hover. Is progressive disclosure of destructive actions a good pattern here, or should delete always be visible?

4. **Edit action**: Clicking the todo title enters inline edit mode. Is this too hidden? Should we have an explicit "edit" affordance?

5. **Completed todos**: Strikethrough + muted color. They stay in the list but visually recede. Should completed items move to the bottom of the list, or stay in place?

Your "trunk test" criteria from TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md: a first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating. Does this design support that?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Great questions from Steve Schoger. These are exactly the kind of design decisions that make or break usability. Let me think through each one carefully.

input.content:
Steve, these are great questions. Here's my gut-check on each:

**1. Auth forms: Centered, narrow, single-column.** Yes. This is the convention. Every login form on the internet looks like this. Users know exactly what to do. Toggle between "Sign in" and "Create account" via a link below — perfect. The only thing I'd add: make sure the link text is specific. Not "Click here" but "Don't have an account? Create one" and "Already have an account? Sign in." The user should never have to figure out which form they're on. Passes the test.

**2. Empty state: "No todos yet" + input at top.** Almost. "No todos yet" is fine as a muted message, but the input field is the real hero of the empty state. Make the input field the dominant visual element — it should be obvious that typing in that field is the thing to do. Placeholder text like "What needs to be done?" gives them a nudge. The muted message is secondary. Don't overthink this — the input field speaks for itself if it's prominent enough. No tutorial, no illustration, no onboarding tips. Just the input.

**3. Delete: visible on hover/focus-within, always visible on mobile.** I like the instinct to keep the list clean, but I'm going to push back slightly. Hide-on-hover means the user has to discover that delete exists by accidentally hovering. That's making them think. My recommendation: **always show the delete button, but make it visually quiet.** A small, muted trash icon or X that doesn't compete with the todo text. It's there when you need it, invisible when you're scanning your list. On a todo list with 5-15 items, a small quiet delete affordance per item won't create visual noise. The hover-to-reveal pattern works for apps with dense data tables — for a simple todo list, just show it.

**4. Edit: click the title to enter edit mode.** This is too hidden. Most users will not discover click-to-edit on their own. They'll look for an edit button and not find one. Two options:
- **Option A**: Small edit icon (pencil) next to the delete button. Always visible but quiet, same as delete. Click it, the title becomes an input. This is the safest choice.
- **Option B**: Double-click to edit. But this has no discoverability and doesn't work on mobile.

I'd go with **Option A**. A visible pencil icon is a universal convention. The user sees it, knows what it does, and never has to think. We can also support click-on-text-to-edit as an enhancement for power users, but the pencil icon is the primary affordance.

**5. Completed todos: stay in place or move to bottom?** Stay in place. Here's why: if I complete the third item in my list and it jumps to the bottom, the list just rearranged itself. That's disorienting — "wait, where did everything go?" Things moving around makes users lose their place. Strikethrough + muted color is enough. The completed items visually recede while staying where the user put them. The list feels stable and predictable.

Exception: if the team later builds a "show/hide completed" toggle, completed items could be hidden entirely. But for MVP, keep them in place.

**Summary of my recommendations:**
1. Auth forms — yes, ship it as described, with specific toggle link text
2. Empty state — input field is the hero, muted message is secondary
3. Delete — always visible, visually quiet (muted icon)
4. Edit — visible pencil icon, same quiet treatment as delete
5. Completed — stay in place, strikethrough + muted

Everything passes the trunk test. The user always knows where they are, what they can do, and how to do it. No thinking required.
input.summary: Usability review of all five component decisions

content.text: I should also loop Heydon in on the edit affordance decision, since click-to-edit vs. visible edit button has major accessibility implications.

input.content:
Heydon, heads up — Steve Schoger and I just worked through some component design decisions. Two that directly affect accessibility:

**Delete button: always visible, visually quiet.** I pushed back on hover-to-reveal. The delete affordance will be a small muted icon on every todo item, always visible. This is better for keyboard and screen reader users too — no need to discover a hidden action.

**Edit: visible pencil icon rather than click-to-edit.** Steve originally proposed clicking the todo title to enter edit mode. I flagged that this is not discoverable for most users. We're going with a visible pencil icon instead. This is also much better for you — a clear, focusable button with a label like "Edit [todo title]" that screen readers can announce. Click-to-edit on a text element would be a nightmare for assistive tech.

Both the edit and delete buttons should be proper `<button>` elements with accessible labels. The icons are visual affordances; the labels are for everyone who can't see them.

Wanted to make sure you're aligned before the team starts building these components.
input.summary: Design decisions affecting accessibility: edit and delete

routing.summary: Design decisions affecting accessibility: edit and delete

**Delete button: always visible, visually quiet.** I pushed back on hover-to-reveal. The delete affordance will be a small muted icon on every todo item, always visible. This is better for keyboard and screen reader users too — no need to discover a hidden action.

**Edit: visible pencil icon rather than click-to-edit.** Steve originally proposed clicking the todo title to enter edit mode. I flagged that this is not discoverable for most users. We're going with a visible pencil icon instead. This is also much better for you — a clear, focusable button with a label like "Edit [todo title]" that screen readers can announce. Click-to-edit on a text element would be a nightmare for assistive tech.

Both the edit and delete buttons should be proper `<button>` elements with accessible labels. The icons are visual affordances; the labels are for everyone who can't see them.

Wanted to make sure you're aligned before the team starts building these components.

content.text:
Good. I've given Steve Schoger detailed usability feedback on all five component design decisions and looped Heydon in on the accessibility-critical ones (edit and delete affordances).

The key UX decisions are now documented across my conversations with the team:

**Settled design patterns:**
- Auth forms: centered, single-column, specific toggle link text
- Empty state: input field is the hero, muted "No todos yet" is secondary
- Delete: always visible, visually quiet muted icon — no hover-to-reveal
- Edit: visible pencil icon, not hidden click-to-edit
- Completed todos: stay in place with strikethrough + muted color

I'm in a good holding pattern. Tasks 1-2 are still in progress (Luca on scaffolding). The feature work (Tasks 4-9) is where I'll be most active — reviewing every screen, every label, every error message as they're built.

Nothing more for me to do right now until either a teammate reaches out or feature work begins. I'll check in on progress.

content.content:
#1 [in_progress] Project scaffolding: Cargo.toml, src structure, docker-compose, and configuration (luca-palmieri)
#2 [pending] Walking skeleton: health check endpoint with integration test [blocked by #1]
#3 [completed] Design tokens: CSS custom properties foundation (lea-verou)
#4 [pending] User registration: email/password signup with TDD [blocked by #2]
#5 [pending] User login/logout: session-based authentication with TDD [blocked by #4]
#6 [pending] View todo list and add todo item with TDD [blocked by #5]
#7 [pending] Complete a todo item with TDD [blocked by #6]
#8 [pending] Delete a todo item with TDD [blocked by #6]
#9 [pending] Edit a todo item title with TDD [blocked by #6]

tasks.subject: Project scaffolding: Cargo.toml, src structure, docker-compose, and configuration
Walking skeleton: health check endpoint with integration test
Design tokens: CSS custom properties foundation
User registration: email/password signup with TDD
User login/logout: session-based authentication with TDD
View todo list and add todo item with TDD
Complete a todo item with TDD
Delete a todo item with TDD
Edit a todo item title with TDD

content.text:
Still waiting on Task 1 to complete so the chain can start moving. Everything is blocked on the scaffolding. That's fine — it's the foundation. Once Luca finishes Tasks 1 and 2, the feature pipeline opens up and I'll be reviewing every interaction as it's built.

For now, I'm standing by. The usability guidance is planted with Marty, both Steves, Heydon, Carson, and Kent. When the mob starts building features, I'll be the voice asking "but would the user understand this?" at every step.
